subject: Mathematics
q: If a circle passes through the point $(a, b)$ and cuts the circle $x^2+y^2=p^2$ orthogonally, then the equation of the locus of its centre is
question: If a circle passes through the point $(a, b)$ and cuts the circle $x^2+y^2=p^2$ orthogonally, then the equation of the locus of its centre is
solution:
Let the centre be $(\alpha, \beta)$
$\because$ It cut the circle $\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2=\mathrm{p}^2$ orthogonally
$2(-\alpha) \times 0+2(-\beta) \times 0=c_1-p^2$
$\mathrm{c}_1=\mathrm{p}^2$
Let equation of circle is $x^2+y^2-2 \alpha x-2 \beta y+p^2=0$
It pass through $(a, b) \Rightarrow a^2+b^2-2 \alpha a-2 \beta b+p^2=0$
Locus $\therefore 2 a x+2 b y-\left(a^2+b^2+p^2\right)=0$

subject: Mathematics
q: Two circles with equal radii are intersecting at the points (0,1) and (0,-1) . The tangent at the point (0,1) to one of the circles passes through the centre of the other circle. Then the distance between the centres of these circles is:
question: Two circles with equal radii are intersecting at the points (0,1) and (0,-1) . The tangent at the point (0,1) to one of the circles passes through the centre of the other circle. Then the distance between the centres of these circles is:
solution:
$\because$ Two circles of equal radii intersect each other orthogonally. Then $R$ is mid point of $P Q$.
and $P R=O_{1} R=O_{2} R$ $P R=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{(0-0)^{2}+(1+1)^{2}}=1$ $\therefore$ Distance between centres $=1+1=2$.

q: If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to a point on the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is equal to<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
question: If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to a point on the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is equal to<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
solution:
Given circle x2+y2=1

General coordinate on a given circle is cosθ, sinθ. 

Given, h=cosθ+32⇒cosθ=2h-32  ...i

k=sinθ+22⇒sinθ=2k-1  ...ii

Square i & ii then add we get, 

h-322+k-12=122

⇒r=12

solution:
x2+y2-2x-6y+6=0
centre 1,3

radius =2
Distance between 1,3 and 2,1 is 5

∴ 52+22=r2

⇒ r=3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let circle $C$ be the image of $x^2+y^2-2 x+4 y-4=0$ in the line $2 x-3 y+5=0$ and $A$ be the point on $C$ such that $O A$ is parallel to $x$-axis and $A$ lies on the right hand side of the centre $O$ of $C$. If $B(\alpha, \beta)$, with $\beta \lt 4$, lies on $C$ such that the length of the are $A B$ is $(1 / 6)^{\text {th }}$ of the perimeter of $C$, then $\beta-\sqrt{3} \alpha$ is equal to
question: Let circle $C$ be the image of $x^2+y^2-2 x+4 y-4=0$ in the line $2 x-3 y+5=0$ and $A$ be the point on $C$ such that $O A$ is parallel to $x$-axis and $A$ lies on the right hand side of the centre $O$ of $C$. If $B(\alpha, \beta)$, with $\beta \lt 4$, lies on $C$ such that the length of the are $A B$ is $(1 / 6)^{\text {th }}$ of the perimeter of $C$, then $\beta-\sqrt{3} \alpha$ is equal to
solution:
Centre $(1,-2), r=3$ Reflection of $(1,-2)$ about $2 x-3 y+5=0$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-3}=\frac{-2(2+6+5)}{13}=-2 \\ & x=-3, y=4 \end{aligned}$ Equation of circle ' C ' $C:(x+3)^2+(y-4)^2=9$ A.T.Q.
$\begin{aligned} & \ell(\operatorname{arcAB})=\frac{1}{6} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r} \\ & \mathrm{r} \theta=\frac{1}{6} \times 2 \pi \mathrm{r} \\ & \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \\ & (\alpha+6)^2+(\beta-4)^2=27 \\ & \frac{(\alpha+3)^2 \pm(\beta-4)^2=9}{(\alpha+6)^2-(\alpha+3)^2=18} \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \alpha=-9\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{-3}{2}, \beta=\left(4-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ & \therefore \beta-\sqrt{3} \alpha \\ & \left(4-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & =4\end{aligned}$

q: If the circles <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>164</mn><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> intersect at two distinct points, then:
question: If the circles <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>164</mn><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> intersect at two distinct points, then:
solution:
As we know, if two circles intersect each other, then

r1-r2<C1C2<r1+r2 ........(i)

Now for the first circle C18, 10 and r1=r

For the second circle C24, 7 and r2=6

From (i)

r-6<5<r+6

⇒r-6<5    ...ii & 5<r+6   ...iii  r-6=r-6, r≥66-r, r<6

from iii r>-1   ...iv

from ii 

when -1<r<6 then 6-r<5⇒1<r ...v

when r≥6⇒r-6<5⇒r<11 ...vi

from iv, v, vi we get

⇒ r∈1, 11

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider a family of circles which are passing through the point $(-1,1)$ and are tangent to $x-$ axis. If $(h, k)$ are the co-ordinates of the centre of the circles, then the set of values of $k$ is given by the interval
question: Consider a family of circles which are passing through the point $(-1,1)$ and are tangent to $x-$ axis. If $(h, k)$ are the co-ordinates of the centre of the circles, then the set of values of $k$ is given by the interval
solution:
Equation of circle $(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=k^2$ 
It is passing through $(-1,1)$ then
$\begin{aligned}
& (-1-h)^2+(1-k)^2=k^2 \\
& h^2+2 h-2 k+2=0 \\
& D \geq 0 \\
& 2 k-1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow k \geq 1 / 2.
\end{aligned}$

question: If the angle of intersection at a point where the two circles with radii <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><mn>12</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> intersect is <math><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the length (in cm) of their common chord is:
solution:
∵C1P=5
C2P=12 & ∠C1PC2=90o
∴C1C2=13
∴ ΔCC2P
tan⁡θ=125⇒sin⁡θ=1213
C1C2 biscets the PQ at R & C1C2⊥PQ
in ΔPC1R,  sin θ=PR5
⇒1213=PR5⇒PR=6013
& PQ=2PR=12013

question: The point diametrically opposite to the point $P(1,0)$ on the circle $x^2+y^2+2 x+4 y-3=0$ is
solution:
Centre $(-1,-2)$
Let $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the required point $\frac{\alpha+1}{2}=-1$ and $\frac{\beta+0}{2}=-2$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the locus of the mid points of the chords of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> drawn from the origin intersect the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then, the length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is:
question: Let the locus of the mid points of the chords of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> drawn from the origin intersect the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then, the length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is:
solution:
Let the mid point of the chords be x1,y1.

The given equation of circle is x2+y-12=1.

⇒x2+y2-2y=0

⇒xx1+yy1-y+y1=x12+y12-2y1

This locus passes through the origin,

⇒0+0-0+y1=x12+y12-2y1

⇒-y1=x12+y12-2y1

⇒x12+y12-y1=0

So, the locus is x2+y2-y=0

It intersects with the line x+y=1.

⇒1-y2+y2-y=0

⇒1+y2-2y+y2-y=0

⇒2y2-2y-y+1=0

⇒2yy-1-y-1=0

⇒2y-1y-1=0

⇒y=1,12

⇒P≡0,1 and Q12,12

⇒PQ=0-122+1-122

⇒PQ=14+14

⇒PQ=12

subject: Mathematics
q: If the circles $x^2+y^2+2 a x+c y+a=0$ and $x^2+y^2-3 a x+d y-1=0$ intersect in two distinct points $P$ and $Q$ then the line $5 x+$ by $-a=0$ passes through $P$ and $Q$ for
question: If the circles $x^2+y^2+2 a x+c y+a=0$ and $x^2+y^2-3 a x+d y-1=0$ intersect in two distinct points $P$ and $Q$ then the line $5 x+$ by $-a=0$ passes through $P$ and $Q$ for
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& S_1=x^2+y^2+2 a x+c y+a=0 \\
& S_2=x^2+y^2-3 a x+d y-1=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Equation of radical axis of $S_1$ and $S_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& S_1-S_2=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 5 a x+(c-d) y+a+1=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Given that $5 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{by}-\mathrm{a}=0$ passes through $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{a}{1}=\frac{c-d}{b}=\frac{a+1}{-a} \\
& \Rightarrow a+1=-a^2 \\
& a^2+a+1=0
\end{aligned}
$$
No real value of a.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the points of intersection of the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+23=0$ and $4 x^{2}+2 y^{2}-20 x-12 y+35=0$ lie on a circle of radius $r$ and centre $(a, b)$, then the value of $a b+18 r^{2}$ is
question: If the points of intersection of the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+23=0$ and $4 x^{2}+2 y^{2}-20 x-12 y+35=0$ lie on a circle of radius $r$ and centre $(a, b)$, then the value of $a b+18 r^{2}$ is
solution:
The circle through intersection of the ellipses is $S_1 + \lambda S_2 = 0$.
$(1+4\lambda)x^2 + (2+2\lambda)y^2 - (6+20\lambda)x - (12+12\lambda)y + (23+35\lambda) = 0$.
For a circle: $1+4\lambda = 2+2\lambda \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2}$.
Circle: $3x^2 + 3y^2 - 16x - 18y + \frac{81}{2} = 0 \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 - \frac{16}{3}x - 6y + \frac{27}{2} = 0$.
Centre $(a,b) = \left(\frac{8}{3}, 3\right)$, $r^2 = \frac{64}{9} + 9 - \frac{27}{2} = \frac{47}{18}$.
$ab + 18r^2 = \frac{8}{3} \times 3 + 18 \times \frac{47}{18} = 8 + 47 = 55$.

subject: Mathematics
q: A rectangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with end points of the one of its sides as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is inscribed in a circle. If the equation of a diameter of the circle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is _____.
question: A rectangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with end points of the one of its sides as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is inscribed in a circle. If the equation of a diameter of the circle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Equation of line AB will be 

y-2=2x-1⇒2x-y=0

Now the given diameter is parallel to AB

Then, the distance between diameter and line AB

=422+12=45

Thus, BC=2×45=85

Also AB=1-32+2-62=20=25

Area of the rectangle=AB×BC=85×25=16

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi></math> pass through the point of the intersection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>(in the first quadrant)of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. Let the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> touch two circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of equal radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. If the centres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> lie on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis, then the square of the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is equal to _________.
question: Let the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>:</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi></math> pass through the point of the intersection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>(in the first quadrant)of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. Let the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> touch two circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of equal radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. If the centres <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> lie on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis, then the square of the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is equal to _________.
solution:
Given: x2+y2=3 and x2=2y

⇒y2+2y−3=0

⇒y+3y−1=0

⇒y=1,-3 Rejected as it gives imaginary value of x

⇒y=1

⇒x=2

⇒P≡2,1

Now, P lies on the line 2x+y=α.

⇒22+1=α

⇒α=3

For circle C1, Q1 lies on y-axis.

Let Q1≡0,β and R1=23 (given)

Line L act as tangent so applying the condition of tangency

⇒P=r

⇒β−33=23

⇒β−3=6

⇒β−3=6, -6

⇒β=9, -3

So, Q1≡0,9 & Q2≡0,-3

⇒ArΔPQ1Q2=1220019−3111

⇒ArΔPQ1Q2=12212

⇒ArΔPQ1Q2=62

⇒ΔPQ1Q22=72

q: Let $y=x$ be the equation of a chord of the circle $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ (in the closed half-plane $x \geq 0$) of diameter 10 passing through the origin. Let $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ be another circle described on the given chord as its diameter. If the equation of the chord of the circle $\mathrm{C}_{2}$, which passes through the point $(2,3)$ and is farthest from the center of $\mathrm{C}_{2}$, is $x+a y+b=0$, then $a-b$ is equal to
question: Let $y=x$ be the equation of a chord of the circle $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ (in the closed half-plane $x \geq 0$) of diameter 10 passing through the origin. Let $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ be another circle described on the given chord as its diameter. If the equation of the chord of the circle $\mathrm{C}_{2}$, which passes through the point $(2,3)$ and is farthest from the center of $\mathrm{C}_{2}$, is $x+a y+b=0$, then $a-b$ is equal to
solution:
Circle $C_1$ has radius 5 and chord on line $y = x$ through origin.
Setting center at $(5, 0)$ (in region $x \geq 0$), the chord endpoints are $(0, 0)$ and $(5, 5)$ by solving $(t-5)^2 + t^2 = 25$.
Circle $C_2$ has this chord as diameter, so center is at $(\frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2})$ with radius $\frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
The chord of $C_2$ through $(2, 3)$ that is farthest from center is perpendicular to the radial direction from center to $(2, 3)$.
The radial direction is $(2 - \frac{5}{2}, 3 - \frac{5}{2}) = (-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2})$ with normal direction $(1, -1)$.
The chord equation is $1(x - 2) - 1(y - 3) = 0$, giving $x - y + 1 = 0$.
In form $x + ay + b = 0$, we have $a = -1$ and $b = 1$, so $a - b = -1 - 1 = -2$.

solution:
Slope of tangent to x2+y2=1 at 12,12
x2+y2=1
2x+2yy'=0
y'=-xy=-1
Given that, y=mx+c, is perpendicular to L1,
So, m=1 ⇒y=x+c

Now distance of 3,0 from y=x+c is
c+32=1
c2+6c+9=2
c2+6c+7=0.

q:
Let

 A=x,y∈R×R∣2x2+2y2-2x-2y=1

B=x,y∈R×R∣4x2+4y2-16y+7=0 and

C=x,y∈R×R∣x2+y2-4x-2y+5≤r2. Then the minimum value of r such that A∪B⊆C is equal to
question:
Let

 A=x,y∈R×R∣2x2+2y2-2x-2y=1

B=x,y∈R×R∣4x2+4y2-16y+7=0 and

C=x,y∈R×R∣x2+y2-4x-2y+5≤r2. Then the minimum value of r such that A∪B⊆C is equal to
solution:
Given,

S1:x2+y2-x-y-12=0

So, the centre is C1:12, 12 and the radius is r1=14+14+12=1

S2:x2+y2-4y+74=0

So, the centre is C2:0,2 and the radius is 

r2=4-74=32

S3:x2+y2-4x-2y+5-r2=0

So, the centre is C3:2,1 and the radius is 

r3=4+1-5+r2=r

From the diagram, we can say that if A∪B⊆C, then the circle A & B should lie in the circle C

Here, C1C3=52

So, 52≤r-1⇒r≤1-52r≥1+52

Also, C2C3=5≤r-32

⇒r≥5+32r≤32-5

So, the minimum value of r=3+252

q: A square is inscribed in the circle $x^2+y^2-10 x-6 y+30=0$. One side of this square is parallel to $y=x+3$. If $\left(x_i, y_i\right)$ are the vertices of the square, then $\mathbf{\Sigma}\left(x_i^2+y_i^2\right)$ is equal to:
question: A square is inscribed in the circle $x^2+y^2-10 x-6 y+30=0$. One side of this square is parallel to $y=x+3$. If $\left(x_i, y_i\right)$ are the vertices of the square, then $\mathbf{\Sigma}\left(x_i^2+y_i^2\right)$ is equal to:
solution: $y=x+c \quad \&$ $\begin{array}{ll} y=x+c \quad \& & x+y+d=0 \\ \left|\frac{5-3+c}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\sqrt{2} & \left|\frac{8+d}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\sqrt{2} \\ |c+2|=2 & 8+d= \pm 2 \\ c=0,-4 & d=-10,-6 \end{array}$ pts $(5,5),(3,3),(7,3),(5,1)$ $\begin{aligned} & \sum\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^2+\mathrm{y}_1^2\right)=25+25+9+9+49+9+25+1 \\ & =152 \end{aligned}$

q: In the circle given below, let OA=1 unit, OB=13 unit and PQ⊥OB. Then, the area of the triangle PQB (in square units) is :
question: In the circle given below, let OA=1 unit, OB=13 unit and PQ⊥OB. Then, the area of the triangle PQB (in square units) is :
solution:
Since x-axis is normal to the circle at O, and normal to the circle passes through its centre. So, OB is a diameter.

Now, perpendicular from centre of circle to chord bisects the chord, so PA=AQ=λ (Let)

Also, product of segments of chords in a circle is equal i.e. OA·AB=AP·AQ

⇒1·12=λ·λ

⇒λ=23 units

Now, area of ΔPQB=12×PQ×AB=12×2λ×AB

i.e. Δ=12·43×12 sq. units

=243 sq. units

q: Let the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> lie inside the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><msqrt><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If the set of all values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Let the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> lie inside the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><msqrt><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> If the set of all values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given,

The point p,p+1 lie inside the region E=x,y:3-x≤y≤9-x2,0≤x≤3,

And the set of all values of p is the interval a,b, 

Now by observation p,p+1 lies on y-x=1,

Also point p,p+1 lies on x+y=3

So, solving y-x=1 and x+y=3 we get, P1,2

Again given point p,p+1 lies on x2+y2=9

So, solving y-x=1 and x2+y2=9, we get

x2+(1+x)2=9

⇒2x2+2x-8=0

⇒x2+x-4=0.........i

⇒x=-1±1+4·42=17-12

Hence, p∈1,17-12

Now on comparing with p∈a,b we get,

⇒a=1, b2+b=4   [using i]

Hence, the value of b2+b-a2=4-1=3

q: The number of common tangents, to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>131</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is
question: The number of common tangents, to the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>131</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is
solution:
If S≡x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0.

C≡-g,-f and r=g2+f2-c.

The given circles are x2+y2-18x-15y+131=0

⇒C19, 152, r1=81+2254-131=52

and x2+y2-6x-6y-7=0

⇒C23, 3, r2=9+9+7=5

⇒d=C1C2=9-32+152-32=36+814=152

⇒r1+r2=52+5=152

⇒C1C2=r1+r2

∴ Circles touch each other externally, 3 common tangents.

Hence this is the correct option.

question: Let a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> lie below the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> passes through the centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and intersects the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. The line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> touches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>51</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
solution:
Plotting the diagram of given data, we get

Finding intersection point of 4x+3y+2=0 and 3x-4y-11=0 we get 1,-2

Now finding the point P by parametric we get,

x-1cosθ=y+2sinθ=±5

y=-2+5-45=-6

x=1+535=4

Now finding distance of point P from line 5x-12y+51=0=54-12-6+5113

=20+72+5113

=14313=11

q: Let a circle $C$ of radius 1 and closer to the origin be such that the lines passing through the point $(3,2)$ and parallel to the coordinate axes touch it. Then the shortest distance of the circle $\mathrm{C}$ from the point $(5,5)$ is :
question: Let a circle $C$ of radius 1 and closer to the origin be such that the lines passing through the point $(3,2)$ and parallel to the coordinate axes touch it. Then the shortest distance of the circle $\mathrm{C}$ from the point $(5,5)$ is :
solution: Coordinates of the centre will be $(2,1)$ Equation of circle will be $\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{x}-2)^2+(\mathrm{y}-1)^2=1 \\ & \mathrm{QC}=\sqrt{(5-2)^2+(5-1)^2} \\ & \mathrm{QC}=5 \end{aligned}$ shortest distance $\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{RQ}=\mathrm{CQ}-\mathrm{CR} \\ & =5-1 \\ & =4 \end{aligned}$

q: Let the centre of a circle, passing through the points $(0,0),(1,0)$ and touching the circle $x^2+y^2=9$, be $(h, k)$. Then for all possible values of the coordinates of the centre $(h, k), 4\left(h^2+k^2\right)$ is equal to_________
question: Let the centre of a circle, passing through the points $(0,0),(1,0)$ and touching the circle $x^2+y^2=9$, be $(h, k)$. Then for all possible values of the coordinates of the centre $(h, k), 4\left(h^2+k^2\right)$ is equal to_________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{h})^2+(\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{k})^2=\mathrm{h}^2+\mathrm{k}^2 \\ & \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2-2 \mathrm{hx}-2 \mathrm{ky}=0 \\ & \because \text { passes through }(1,0) \\ & \Rightarrow 1+0-2 \mathrm{~h}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=1 / 2 \\ & \because \mathrm{OC}=\frac{\mathrm{OP}}{2} \\ & \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\mathrm{k}^2}=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{4}+\mathrm{k}^2=\frac{9}{4} \\ & \mathrm{k}^2=2 \\ & \mathrm{k}= \pm \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Possible coordinate of $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{c}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})\left(\frac{1}{2}, \sqrt{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2},-\sqrt{2}\right) \\ & 4\left(\mathrm{~h}^2+\mathrm{k}^2\right)=4\left(\frac{1}{4}+2\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)=9 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Two tangents are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is a point on the circle such that length of the segments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are equal, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is equal to:
question: Two tangents are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is a point on the circle such that length of the segments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are equal, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given, the equation of circle is x2+y2-2x-6y+6=0

So, the centre of the circle is 1,3 and the radius is 12+32-6=2

Assume, the coordinate of point B is -1,k and the coordinate of point A is h,1.

So, h+12=2⇒h=1 and k-12=2⇒k=3

So, the coordinate of B is -1,3 and the coordinate of A is 1,1.

From the diagram, area of ∆ABD=12×2×4

=4 sq. units

subject: Mathematics
q: A circle passing through the point Pα,β in the first quadrant touches the two coordinate axes at the points A and B. The point P is above the line AB. The point Q on the line segment AB is the foot of perpendicular from P on AB. If PQ is equal to 11 units, then the value of αβ is _______
question: A circle passing through the point Pα,β in the first quadrant touches the two coordinate axes at the points A and B. The point P is above the line AB. The point Q on the line segment AB is the foot of perpendicular from P on AB. If PQ is equal to 11 units, then the value of αβ is _______
solution:
Let the equation of circle touching both the coordinate axis be,

(x-r)2+(y-r)2=r2 which passes through α,β

So, (α-r)2+(β-r)2=r2 .......1

Also equation of line AB: x+y=r

 

Let Qh,k,

Now using the formula of foot of perpendicular,

h-α1=k-β1=-(α+β-r)2

⇒(h,k)=α-α2-β2+r2,β-α2-β2+r2

⇒h,k=α-β+r2,-α+β+r2

Now using the distance formula we get,

PQ2=-α-β+r22+-α-β+r22=121

⇒(r-(α+β))2=242

⇒r2+(α+β)2-2r(α+β)=242

⇒α2+β2+2αβ+r2-2rα-2rβ=242

⇒2αβ=242 from equation 1, (α-r)2+(β-r)2=r2

⇒αβ=121

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the tangents at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></math> on the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Then the radius of the circle, whose centre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and the line joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is its tangent, is equal to
question: Let the tangents at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>)</mo></math> on the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, intersect at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Then the radius of the circle, whose centre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and the line joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is its tangent, is equal to
solution:
Given,

The tangents at the points A(4,-11) and B(8,-5) on the circle x2+y2-3x+10y-15=0, intersect at the point C. 

So, equation of tangent at A(4,-11) on circle will be

⇒4x-11y-3x+42+10y-112-15=0

⇒5x-12y-152=0 .........1

And equation of tangent at B(8,-5) on circle is

⇒8x-5y-3x+82+10y-52-15=0

⇒13x-104=0⇒x=8 .........2

Now putting the value of x=8 in equation 1 we get, y=-283

So, the point C will be C8,-283

Now equation of tangent AB will be,

y+11=-5+118-4x-4

⇒3x-2y-34=0

r=3×8+2×283-3413=-26313=2133
